title: Data preparation
text: first step load raw species occurrence data environmental raster layers. ’s always good practice visualize spatial distribution points inspect environmental layers.
code: # Load the raw occurrence data from the package occ_file <- system.file("extdata", "Rusa_unicolor.csv", package = "bean") occ_data_raw <- read.csv(occ_file)  # Display the first few rows to understand its structure head(occ_data_raw) #>         species        y         x #> 1 Rusa unicolor 15.37239  99.11555 #> 2 Rusa unicolor 15.41415  99.28763 #> 3 Rusa unicolor 14.46838 101.22005 #> 4 Rusa unicolor 15.65606  99.31600 #> 5 Rusa unicolor 14.39543 101.41694 #> 6 Rusa unicolor 12.72500 100.88947 # Load the environmental raster layers thai_env_file <- system.file("extdata", "thai_env.tif", package = "bean") env <- terra::rast(c(thai_env_file))  # Plot the environmental layers to check their extent and values plot(env, mar = c(1, 1, 2, 4)) # Visualize the spatial distribution of the occurrence points ggplot(occ_data_raw, aes(x = x, y = y)) +   geom_raster(data = as.data.frame(env[[1]], xy = TRUE), aes(x = x, y = y), fill = "gray80") +   geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "darkred") +   coord_fixed() +   labs(title = "Sambar Deer Occurrence Points") +   theme_classic()

what: Step 2: Prepare Data for Environmental Thinning
title: Data preparation
text: analysis, raw data must cleaned standardized. prepare_bean() function streamlines process : Removing records missing coordinates. Extracting environmental data point raster layers. Removing records fall outside raster extent. ensures subsequent functions work clean, complete, scaled dataset.
code: # Run the preparation function to clean and scale the data origin_dat_prepared <- prepare_bean(   data = occ_data_raw,   env_rasters = env,   longitude = "x",   latitude = "y",   transform = "none" ) #> Skipping raster transformation. #> Extracting environmental data for occurrence points... #> 5 records removed because they fell outside the raster extent or had NA environmental values. #> Data preparation complete. Returning 1024 clean records.  # View the structure and summary of the clean, scaled data head(origin_dat_prepared) #>         species        y         x    bio_1 bio_12   bio_15    bio_4 #> 1 Rusa unicolor 15.37239  99.11555 23.66463   1414 78.85445 175.0943 #> 2 Rusa unicolor 15.41415  99.28763 24.87193   1289 78.20957 180.2106 #> 3 Rusa unicolor 14.46838 101.22005 24.11074   1166 77.36796 173.1696 #> 4 Rusa unicolor 15.65606  99.31600 25.22871   1309 78.92366 186.1482 #> 5 Rusa unicolor 14.39543 101.41694 23.16356   1134 76.03447 182.5639 #> 6 Rusa unicolor 12.72500 100.88947 27.70646   1269 74.36774 123.1260 summary(origin_dat_prepared) #>       species           y                x             bio_1       #>  Length   :1024   Min.   : 7.508   Min.   : 98.6   Min.   :22.07   #>  N.unique :   1   1st Qu.:14.414   1st Qu.:101.2   1st Qu.:23.16   #>  N.blank  :   0   Median :14.450   Median :101.3   Median :24.11   #>  Min.nchar:  13   Mean   :14.468   Mean   :101.2   Mean   :24.47   #>  Max.nchar:  13   3rd Qu.:14.539   3rd Qu.:101.4   3rd Qu.:25.08   #>                   Max.   :18.826   Max.   :103.5   Max.   :28.31   #>      bio_12         bio_15          bio_4        #>  Min.   : 806   Min.   :53.19   Min.   : 69.86   #>  1st Qu.:1134   1st Qu.:76.03   1st Qu.:173.17   #>  Median :1159   Median :77.37   Median :182.56   #>  Mean   :1191   Mean   :76.98   Mean   :179.68   #>  3rd Qu.:1176   3rd Qu.:77.57   3rd Qu.:188.16   #>  Max.   :2803   Max.   :91.24   Max.   :268.08  # Visualize the spatial distribution of the cleaned occurrence points ggplot(origin_dat_prepared, aes(x = x, y = y)) +   geom_raster(data = as.data.frame(env[[1]], xy = TRUE), aes(x = x, y = y), fill = "gray80") +   geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "darkred") +   coord_fixed() +   labs(title = "Sambar Deer Occurrence Points (Cleaned)") +   theme_classic()

what: Step 3: Objective Grid Resolution using Nearest Neighbors
title: Environmental thinning
text: critical parameter environmental gridding grid_resolution. Instead guessing value, can derive objectively data analyzing density points environmental space. find_env_resolution() function uses geometric “elbow” method based nearest-neighbor distances. identifies exact distance dense artificial clustering transitions natural data spacing, eliminating need arbitrary quantiles.
code: data(origin_dat_prepared, package = "bean") # Find the objective resolution using the elbow method resolution_results <- find_env_resolution(   data = origin_dat_prepared,   env_vars = c("bio_1", "bio_4", "bio_12", "bio_15") ) #> Calculating nearest neighbor environmental distances and detecting elbows...  plot(resolution_results) # Let's use this objective resolution in the next step grid_res <- resolution_results$suggested_resolution grid_res #>       bio_1       bio_4      bio_12      bio_15  #> 0.002405167 0.098876953 1.000000000 0.034934998

title: Environmental thinning
text: Now objective, data-driven grid_resolution, can apply thinning. offer two methods: stochastic deterministic.

what: Method A: Stochastic Thinning with thin_env_nd
title: Environmental thinning
text: method randomly samples exactly one point occupied grid cell, enforcing strict presence-logic within environmental hypercube.
code: # Apply the stochastic thinning (using a seed for reproducibility) thinned_stochastic <- thin_env_nd(   data = origin_dat_prepared,   env_vars = c("bio_1","bio_4", "bio_12", "bio_15"),   grid_resolution = grid_res )  # Print the summary of the thinning results thinned_stochastic #> --- Bean Stochastic Thinning Results --- #>  #> Thinned 1024 original points to 78 points. #> This represents a retention of 7.6% of the data. #>  #> -------------------------------------- head(thinned_stochastic$thinned_data) #>           species        y         x    bio_1 bio_12   bio_15    bio_4 #> 945 Rusa unicolor 14.45169 101.29557 24.11074   1166 77.36796 173.1696 #> 807 Rusa unicolor 16.45394 101.72157 24.19963   1010 80.99183 225.2455 #> 968 Rusa unicolor 16.25104 101.57840 24.35942   1031 80.01744 209.8779 #> 919 Rusa unicolor 16.29003 101.49113 24.40832   1048 80.30025 207.4802 #> 812 Rusa unicolor 15.67431  99.28283 24.51126   1315 78.66610 186.7147 #> 484 Rusa unicolor 12.79893  99.45413 24.52396   1115 73.28954 110.6435

what: Method B: Deterministic Thinning with thin_env_center
title: Environmental thinning
text: method provides simpler, non-random alternative. returns single new point exact center every occupied grid cell, regardless many original points fell within .
code: # Apply the deterministic thinning thinned_deterministic <- thin_env_center(   data = origin_dat_prepared,   env_vars = c("bio_1","bio_4", "bio_12", "bio_15"),   grid_resolution = c(0.5,0.5,0.5,0.5) )  # Print the summary of the thinning results thinned_deterministic #> --- Bean Deterministic Thinning Results --- #>  #> Thinned 1024 original points to 78 unique grid cell centers. #> This represents a retention of 7.6% of the data. #>  #> -------------------------------------- head(thinned_deterministic$thinned_points) #>   bio_1  bio_4  bio_12 bio_15 #> 1 23.75 175.25 1414.25  78.75 #> 2 24.75 180.25 1289.25  78.25 #> 3 24.25 173.25 1166.25  77.25 #> 4 25.25 186.25 1309.25  78.75 #> 5 23.25 182.75 1134.25  76.25 #> 6 27.75 123.25 1269.25  74.25

title: Niche modeling
text: plot_bean() function provides powerful way visualize effect thinning overlaying thinned points original data within environmental grid.
code: data(occ_data_raw, package = "bean") data(origin_dat_prepared, package = "bean") data(thinned_stochastic, package = "bean") data(thinned_deterministic, package = "bean")  # Visualize the stochastic thinning results plot_bean(   original_data = origin_dat_prepared,   thinned_object = thinned_stochastic,   env_vars = c("bio_1","bio_4", "bio_12", "bio_15") ) # Visualize the deterministic thinning results plot_bean(   original_data = origin_dat_prepared,   thinned_object = thinned_deterministic,   env_vars = c("bio_1","bio_4", "bio_12", "bio_15") ) # Visualize the spatial distribution of the occurrence and thinned points  # Load the environmental raster layers thai_env_file <- system.file("extdata", "thai_env.tif", package = "bean") env <- terra::rast(c(thai_env_file))  # Combine the data and add a label for the partition plot_data <- dplyr::bind_rows(   occ_data_raw %>% dplyr::mutate(Data_Type = "Original (Raw)"),   thinned_stochastic$thinned_data %>% dplyr::mutate(Data_Type = "Thinned (Stochastic)") )  # Lock the order so "Original" is always on the left partition plot_data$Data_Type <- factor(plot_data$Data_Type, levels = c("Original (Raw)", "Thinned (Stochastic)"))  # Plot with a partition (facet_wrap) ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = x, y = y)) +   geom_raster(data = as.data.frame(env[[1]], xy = TRUE), aes(x = x, y = y), fill = "gray80") +   geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "darkred") +   coord_fixed() +   facet_wrap(~Data_Type) + # <--- This creates the partition line!   labs(title = "Spatial Point Distribution",        x = "Longitude", y = "Latitude") +   theme_classic() +   theme(strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey90"),         strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))

what: Step 6: Delineate and Visualize the Niche Ellipse
title: Niche modeling
text: thinning, can formalize environmental niche fitting bivariate ellipse around points. fit_ellipsoid() function delineates boundary.

previous_headings: Step 6: Delineate and Visualize the Niche Ellipse
title: Niche modeling
text: 3D Stochastic Ellipsoid
code: # Fit an ellipse that contains 95% of the original data origin_ellipse <- fit_ellipsoid(data = origin_dat_prepared,                                  env_vars = c("bio_1","bio_4", "bio_12", "bio_15"),                                  method = "covmat",                                  level = 0.95) # The returned object contains all the details origin_ellipse #> --- Bean Environmental Niche Ellipsoid --- #>  #> Method: 'covmat'. #> Fitted in 4 dimensions to 1024 data points at a 95.00% level. #> 947 out of 1024 points (92.5%) fall within the ellipsoid boundary. #>  #> Niche Centroid: #>      bio_1      bio_4     bio_12     bio_15  #>   24.47107  179.68260 1191.05273   76.98212  # And we can use the custom plot() method for a powerful visualization plot(origin_ellipse, dims = c(1, 2)) # For interactive 3D:  plot(origin_ellipse, dims = c(1, 2, 3))

previous_headings: Step 6: Delineate and Visualize the Niche Ellipse
title: Niche modeling
text: 3D Stochastic Ellipsoid
code: # Fit an ellipse that contains 95% of the thinned data stochastic_ellipse <- fit_ellipsoid(data = thinned_stochastic$thinned_data,                                      env_vars = c("bio_1","bio_4", "bio_12", "bio_15"),                                      method = "covmat",                                      level = 0.95) stochastic_ellipse #> --- Bean Environmental Niche Ellipsoid --- #>  #> Method: 'covmat'. #> Fitted in 4 dimensions to 78 data points at a 95.00% level. #> 71 out of 78 points (91.0%) fall within the ellipsoid boundary. #>  #> Niche Centroid: #>      bio_1      bio_4     bio_12     bio_15  #>   25.63890  167.99191 1323.07692   76.97539  plot(stochastic_ellipse, dims = c(1, 2)) # For interactive 3D:  plot(stochastic_ellipse, dims = c(1, 2, 3))

previous_headings: Step 6: Delineate and Visualize the Niche Ellipse
title: Niche modeling
text: 3D Deterministic Ellipsoid
code: # Fit an ellipse that contains 95% of the thinned data deterministic_ellipse <- fit_ellipsoid(data = thinned_deterministic$thinned_points,                                        env_vars = c("bio_1","bio_4", "bio_12", "bio_15"),                                         method = "covmat",                                         level = 0.95) deterministic_ellipse #> --- Bean Environmental Niche Ellipsoid --- #>  #> Method: 'covmat'. #> Fitted in 4 dimensions to 78 data points at a 95.00% level. #> 72 out of 78 points (92.3%) fall within the ellipsoid boundary. #>  #> Niche Centroid: #>      bio_1      bio_4     bio_12     bio_15  #>   25.66026  168.01923 1323.32692   76.98718  plot(deterministic_ellipse, dims = c(1, 2)) # For interactive 3D:  plot(deterministic_ellipse, dims = c(1, 2, 3))

title: Niche modeling
text: Finally, project learned fundamental niche back geographic space. predict method calculates continuous Mahalanobis distance suitability scores across landscape.
code: # Predict using the unthinned ellipsoid origin_pred <- predict(object = origin_ellipse,                        newdata = env,                        include_suitability = TRUE,                        suitability_truncated = TRUE,                        include_mahalanobis = TRUE,                        mahalanobis_truncated = TRUE) plot(origin_pred) # Predict using the stochastic thinned ellipsoid stochastic_pred <- predict(object = stochastic_ellipse,                            newdata = env,                            include_suitability = TRUE,                            suitability_truncated = TRUE,                            include_mahalanobis = TRUE,                            mahalanobis_truncated = TRUE) plot(stochastic_pred) # Predict using the deterministic thinned ellipsoid deterministic_pred <- predict(object = deterministic_ellipse,                               newdata = env,                               include_suitability = TRUE,                               suitability_truncated = TRUE,                               include_mahalanobis = TRUE,                               mahalanobis_truncated = TRUE) plot(deterministic_pred)

what: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space
title: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space
text: Paanwaris Paansri Luis E. Escobar

title: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space
text: bean package provides tool address fundamental challenge species distribution modeling (SDM, ecological niche modeling, ENM): sampling bias. Occurrence records species rarely collected systematic, stratified process. Instead, often cluster easily accessible areas (like roads cities) well-studied research sites. spatial bias can translate environmental bias, model incorrectly learns species associated environmental conditions heavily sampled areas, rather true ecological requirements. bean tackles problem thinning occurrence data environmental space. goal create uniform distribution points across species’ observed environmental niche, reducing influence densely clustered records. allows construction accurate fundamental niche volume, can projected geographic space create less biased prediction area environmental suitability. name bean reflects core principle method: ensuring “pod” (grid cell environmental space) contains specified number “beans” (occurrence points).

title: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space
text: bean operates shifting focus geographic space environmental space: Environmental Gridding: Divides environmental hypercube “pods”. Objective Thinning: Reduces clusters specified density per pod. Niche Delineation: Fits ellipsoids thinned data define fundamental niche. Projection: Maps corrected niche back geographic space less biased predictions.

title: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space
text: development version bean can installed GitHub: load package:
code: # Install devtools if needed if (!require("devtools")) install.packages("devtools")  # Install bean devtools::install_github("paanwaris/bean") library(bean)

title: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space
text: typical bean workflow consists key steps:

title: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space
text: prepare_bean() function cleans raw occurrence data removing missing coordinates extracting environmental values raster layers. ensures subsequent analyses use clean, scaled dataset.

title: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space
text: Instead arbitrary thinning, find_env_resolution() uses geometric “elbow” method based nearest-neighbor distances E-space. identifies exact distance dense artificial clustering transitions natural data spacing.

title: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space
text: bean offers two core thinning methods: Stochastic (thin_env_nd): Randomly samples one “bean” occupied “pod”. Deterministic (thin_env_center): Generates new point exact center every occupied grid cell.

title: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space
text: fit_ellipsoid() function formalizes environmental niche fitting bivariate multivariate ellipse around thinned points.

title: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space
text: Using learned niche, predict() projects results back geographic space. step emphasizes algorithms like MaxEnt used calculate suitability scores delineated niche boundaries.

what: bean: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space — bean-package
title: bean: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space — bean-package
text: Provides suite tools mitigate sampling bias species occurrence records thinning data environmental space (E-space). process help increase accuracy precision species distribution modeling (SDM, ecological niche modeling, ENM). package offers data-driven protocol determine thinning parameters. Thinning methods (stochastic deterministic) help reduce oversampled conditions outlier observations. name 'bean' reflects core principle method: ensuring “pod” (grid cell E-space) contains specified number “beans” (occurrence points).

title: bean: Data Thinning of Species Occurrences in Environmental Space — bean-package

what: Find objective environmental resolution using the Nearest Neighbor Elbow Method — find_env_resolution
title: Find objective environmental resolution using the Nearest Neighbor Elbow Method — find_env_resolution
text: Calculates objective, data-driven grid resolution environmental thinning. Instead relying user-defined quantiles, function analyzes 1D Nearest Neighbor (NN) distances environmental variable. uses geometric "elbow" method (point maximum curvature) identify exact distance dense artificial clustering transitions natural data spacing.

title: Find objective environmental resolution using the Nearest Neighbor Elbow Method — find_env_resolution
code: find_env_resolution(data, env_vars)

title: Find objective environmental resolution using the Nearest Neighbor Elbow Method — find_env_resolution
text: data data.frame containing environmental variables. env_vars character vector specifying environmental variables analyze.

title: Find objective environmental resolution using the Nearest Neighbor Elbow Method — find_env_resolution
text: object class bean_env_resolution

title: Outlier removal — fit_ellipsoid
text: function calculates bivariate ellipse encompasses specified proportion data points 2D environmental space. can use either standard covariance matrix robust Minimum Volume Ellipsoid. function also correctly identifies input points fall within outside calculated ellipse boundary, preserving original columns.

title: Outlier removal — fit_ellipsoid
code: fit_ellipsoid(data, env_vars, method = "covmat", level = 0.95)

title: Outlier removal — fit_ellipsoid
text: data data.frame containing species occurrence coordinates environmental variables. env_vars character vector specifying column names data represent environmental variables used analysis. method (character) method calculating centroid covariance matrix. Options "covmat" (standard covariance matrix) "mve" (Minimum Volume Ellipsoid, robust outliers). Default = "covmat". See Details level (numeric) single value 0 1 representing proportion data points ellipse intended encompass. Default 0.95.

title: Outlier removal — fit_ellipsoid
text: object class bean_ellipsoid, list containing: niche_ellipse data.frame points defining perimeter calculated ellipse. centroid named vector representing center ellipse. covariance_matrix 2x2 covariance matrix used define ellipse's shape. all_points_used input data frame, filtered include complete, finite observations. points_in_ellipse data.frame containing subset rows all_points_used fall inside ellipse boundary. points_outside_ellipse data.frame containing subset rows all_points_used fall outside ellipse boundary. inside_indices numeric vector row indices (all_points_used) points inside ellipse. parameters list key parameters used, including level method.

title: Outlier removal — fit_ellipsoid
text: function provides two distinct statistical approaches defining center shape ellipse, selectable via method parameter. size ellipse controlled level parameter, defines statistical confidence interval. ## Method method argument determines centroid covariance matrix (shape orientation) data cloud calculated. - "covmat" (Default): classical approach, uses     standard sample mean sample covariance matrix calculated     provided data points. optimal cleanly distributed, multivariate     normal data, method highly sensitive outliers. single     anomalous data point can significantly skew mean inflate     covariance matrix, resulting ellipse poorly represents     central tendency data (Rousseeuw & Leroy, 2003). - "mve": option uses Minimum Volume Ellipsoid (MVE) estimator,     robust statistical method designed resist influence outliers     (Rousseeuw et al., 1984, 1985). Instead using data points, MVE algorithm finds     ellipsoid smallest possible volume contains specified     subset data (least h = (n_points + n_variables + 1)/2 points)     (Cobos et al., 2024). focusing concentrated "core" data,     MVE method effectively ignores outliers, providing reliable     estimate data's true center scatter contamination     present (Van Aelst & Rousseeuw, 2009). ## Confidence Level level parameter specifies confidence level ellipse, representing percentage data ellipse intended encompass (Cobos et al., 2024). determines size ellipse defining statistical boundary based Mahalanobis distances centroid. Assuming data follows multivariate normal distribution, boundary ellipse corresponds quantile chi-squared (\(\chi^2\)) distribution (Van Aelst & Rousseeuw, 2009). example, level 95 (default) constructs ellipse whose boundary defined set points squared Mahalanobis distance equal 0.95 quantile \(\chi^2\) distribution 2 degrees freedom (2D analysis). Points smaller Mahalanobis distance inside ellipse, larger distance outside. higher level (e.g., 99) result larger ellipse, lower level (e.g., 90) produce smaller, conservative ellipse.

title: Outlier removal — fit_ellipsoid
text: Rousseeuw, P. J., & Leroy, . M. (2003). Robust regression outlier detection. John wiley & sons. Van Aelst, S., & Rousseeuw, P. (2009). Minimum volume ellipsoid. Wiley Interdisciplinary Reviews: Computational Statistics, 1(1), 71-82. Cobos, M.E., Osorio-Olvera, L., Soberón, J., Peterson, .T., Barve, V. & Barve, N. (2024) ellipsenm: ecological niche’s characterizations using ellipsoids. <https://github.com/marlonecobos/ellipsenm> Rousseeuw, P. J. (1984). Least median squares regression. Journal American statistical association, 79(388), 871-880. Rousseeuw, P. J. (1985). Multivariate estimation high breakdown point. Mathematical statistics applications, 8(283-297), 37.

title: Outlier removal — fit_ellipsoid
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # 1. Create environmental data with a cluster and an outlier set.seed(81) env_data <- data.frame(   BIO1 = c(rnorm(50, mean = 10, sd = 1), 30),   BIO12 = c(rnorm(50, mean = 20, sd = 2), 50) )  # 2. Fit a 95% ellipse using the standard covariance method fit <- fit_ellipsoid(   data = env_data,   env_vars = c("BIO1", "BIO12"),   method = "covmat",   level = 0.95 )  # 3. Print the summary and plot the results print(fit) plot(fit) } # }

what: Visualize n-dimensional environmental thinning results — plot_bean
title: Visualize n-dimensional environmental thinning results — plot_bean
text: function creates scatterplot matrix (pairs plot)   visualize results n-dimensional environmental thinning using base R   graphics. can accept thinned objects either density-based thinning   (`thin_env_nd`) deterministic centroid thinning (`thin_env_center`).

code: plot_bean(original_data, thinned_object, env_vars)

title: Visualize n-dimensional environmental thinning results — plot_bean
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Assume 'prepared_data' is ready.  # --- Example: Plotting Density-Based Thinning --- thinned_obj <- thin_env_nd(   data = prepared_data,   env_vars = c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3"),   grid_resolution = c(0.5, 0.5, 0.5) )  plot_bean(   original_data = prepared_data,   thinned_object = thinned_obj,   env_vars = c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3") ) } # }

what: Predict suitability and Mahalanobis distance from a bean ellipsoid — predict.ellipsoid_bean
title: Predict suitability and Mahalanobis distance from a bean ellipsoid — predict.ellipsoid_bean
text: Computes Mahalanobis distance suitability values deriving fitted bean_ellipsoid object new environmental data.

title: Predict suitability and Mahalanobis distance from a bean ellipsoid — predict.ellipsoid_bean
code: # S3 method for class 'bean_ellipsoid' predict(   object,   newdata,   include_suitability = TRUE,   suitability_truncated = FALSE,   include_mahalanobis = TRUE,   mahalanobis_truncated = FALSE,   keep_data = NULL,   ... )

title: Predict suitability and Mahalanobis distance from a bean ellipsoid — predict.ellipsoid_bean
text: object object class bean_ellipsoid. newdata Environmental predictors. Can data.frame, matrix, terra::SpatRaster. Must contain column/layer names matching variables used fit ellipsoid. include_suitability (logical) TRUE (default), returns continuous suitability values. suitability_truncated (logical) TRUE, returns truncated suitability layer values outside chi-square contour set 0. Default = FALSE. include_mahalanobis (logical) TRUE (default), returns continuous Mahalanobis distance. mahalanobis_truncated (logical) TRUE, returns truncated Mahalanobis layer values outside chi-square contour set NA. Default = FALSE. keep_data (logical) TRUE, includes original predictors output. Default FALSE SpatRaster TRUE data.frames. ... Additional arguments (unused).

title: Predict suitability and Mahalanobis distance from a bean ellipsoid — predict.ellipsoid_bean
text: data.frame SpatRaster (matching input type newdata)   containing requested prediction layers.

what: Prepare data for environmental thinning — prepare_bean
title: Prepare data for environmental thinning — prepare_bean
text: function serves pre-processing step clean prepare species occurrence data. performs three key actions: 1. Removes records missing longitude latitude values. 2. Extracts environmental data raster layers already scaled    occurrence point. 3. Removes records fall outside raster extent missing    environmental data. final output clean data frame environmental variables mean 0 standard deviation 1.

title: Prepare data for environmental thinning — prepare_bean
code: prepare_bean(data, env_rasters, longitude, latitude, transform = "scale")

title: Prepare data for environmental thinning — prepare_bean
text: data data.frame species occurrences records, including columns longitude latitude. env_rasters SpatRaster (terra package) RasterStack (raster package) object environmental variables. longitude (character) name longitude column data. latitude (character) name latitude column data. transform (character) transformation apply environmental rasters extracting data. Options "scale" (default), "pca", "none". See Details.

title: Prepare data for environmental thinning — prepare_bean
text: data.frame containing cleaned scaled occurrence data,   following columns: Original Columns columns input data preserved valid records. Environmental Variables New columns, named layers env_rasters, containing extracted scaled environmental data.

title: Prepare data for environmental thinning — prepare_bean
text: ### Environmental Variable Transformation transform argument allows different pre-processing environmental raster layers address issues like differing units multicollinearity. - "scale" (Default):** standard approach handle variables   different units (e.g., °C vs. mm). transforms raster layer   mean 0 standard deviation 1 (Baddeley et al., 2016). process makes   variables equal variance. result,   variable contributes equally analysis, ensuring   resulting resolutions based relative distribution data points   within environmental dimension, arbitrary original units   (Beaugrand, 2024; Kléparski et al., 2021). - "pca": option performs Principal Component Analysis (PCA)   environmental rasters. powerful technique dealing   multicollinearity (highly correlated variables). transforms original   rasters new set uncorrelated layers (Principal Components) (Qiao et al., 2016).   function extracts PC scores occurrence point. - "none": option extracts raw environmental values   rasters without transformation. suitable rasters   already scaled specific reason use raw values.

title: Prepare data for environmental thinning — prepare_bean
text: Baddeley, ., Rubak, E. Turner, R. (2016). Spatial point patterns: methodology applications R. CRC press. Beaugrand, G. (2024). ecological niche model considers local relationships among variables: Environmental String Model. Ecosphere, 15(10), e70015. Kléparski, L., Beaugrand, G. Edwards, M. (2021). Plankton biogeography North Atlantic Ocean adjacent seas: Species assemblages environmental signatures. Ecology Evolution, 11(10), 5135-5149. Qiao, H., Peterson, . T., Campbell, L. P., Soberón, J., Ji, L. Escobar, L. E. (2016). NicheA: creating virtual species ecological niches multivariate environmental scenarios. Ecography, 39(8), 805-813.

title: Prepare data for environmental thinning — prepare_bean
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(terra)  # 1. Load sample data bio1_file <- system.file("extdata", "BIO1.tif", package = "bean") bio12_file <- system.file("extdata", "BIO12.tif", package = "bean") env_rasters <- terra::rast(c(bio1_file, bio12_file))  occ_file <- system.file("extdata", "Peromyscus_maniculatus_original.csv", package = "bean") data <- read.csv(occ_file)  # 2. Run the preparation function prepared_data <- prepare_bean(   data = data,   env_rasters = env_rasters,   longitude = "x",   latitude = "y",   scale = TRUE )  # 3. View the clean, scaled data head(prepared_data) summary(prepared_data) } # }

title: Deterministic centroid — thin_env_center
text: function thins species occurrence records finding occupied cells 2D environmental grid returning single new point exact center cells. deterministic method.

title: Deterministic centroid — thin_env_center
code: thin_env_center(data, env_vars, grid_resolution)

title: Deterministic centroid — thin_env_center
text: data data.frame containing species occurrence coordinates environmental variables. env_vars character vector specifying column names data represent environmental variables used analysis. grid_resolution numeric vector length one two specifying resolution(s) grid axes. length one, used axes.

title: Deterministic centroid — thin_env_center
text: object class bean_thinned_center. list containing: thinned_points data.frame two columns representing new     points center occupied environmental grid cell. n_original integer representing number complete occurrence     records input data. n_thinned integer representing number unique grid cells     occupied, also number points returned. parameters list key parameters used, whether     scaling applied.

title: Deterministic centroid — thin_env_center
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # 1. Create environmental data set.seed(81) env_data <- data.frame( BIO1 = c(0.1, 0.2, 1.1, 1.2, 1.3), BIO12 = c(0.1, 0.2, 2.1, 2.2, 2.3) )  # 2. Thin the data to grid cell centers thinned_center_obj <- thin_env_center(   data = env_data,   env_vars = c("BIO1", "BIO12"),   grid_resolution = c(0.1, 0.2) )  # 3. Print the summary print(thinned_center_obj) } # }

what: Thin occurrence data in n-dimensional environmental space — thin_env_nd
title: Thin occurrence data in n-dimensional environmental space — thin_env_nd
text: function thins species occurrence records n-dimensional environmental space randomly sampling exactly one point occupied n-dimensional grid cell (hypercube).

title: Thin occurrence data in n-dimensional environmental space — thin_env_nd
code: thin_env_nd(data, env_vars, grid_resolution, seed = NULL)

title: Thin occurrence data in n-dimensional environmental space — thin_env_nd
text: data data.frame containing species occurrences pre-scaled environmental variables, typically output `prepare_bean()`. env_vars character vector two column names representing environmental variables (dimensions) use thinning. grid_resolution numeric vector resolutions environmental axis. length must match length `env_vars`. seed (numeric) optional random seed reproducibility. provided, random number generator state safely isolated function call affect global environment. Default = NULL.

title: Thin occurrence data in n-dimensional environmental space — thin_env_nd
text: object class `bean_thinned`, list containing: thinned_data data.frame containing occurrence records retained thinning process. n_original integer representing number complete occurrence records input data thinning. n_thinned integer representing number occurrence records remaining thinning. parameters list key parameters used thinning process.

title: Thin occurrence data in n-dimensional environmental space — thin_env_nd
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Assume 'prepared_pca' is the output from prepare_bean(..., transform = "pca") # and contains columns PC1, PC2, PC3.  # Thin the data in 3D environmental space, retaining 1 point per grid cell thinned_3d <- thin_env_nd(   data = prepared_pca$prepared_data,   env_vars = c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3"),   grid_resolution = c(0.5, 0.5, 0.5), # One resolution value for each axis   seed = 123 )  # Print the summary print(thinned_3d) } # }

title: Deterministic thinned environmental data — thinned_deterministic
text: output object generated running thin_env_center prepared occurrence data. contains single calculated centroid coordinate occupied environmental grid cell.

title: Deterministic thinned environmental data — thinned_deterministic
code: thinned_deterministic

title: Deterministic thinned environmental data — thinned_deterministic
text: object class bean_thinned_center containing: thinned_points data frame new geometric center points n_original number points thinning n_thinned number unique grid cells occupied parameters thinning parameters used
